commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a public `renameIdentity` write option for unstable-inode FUSE mounts, using sidecar locking plus SHA-256 content verification with docs, exports, and tests.

Reproducibility: yes. for the PR defects: source inspection shows the opt-in still reaches the default Python helper path, while every new behavioral test forces Python mode off. I did not run a live rclone mount in this checkout.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Manual review is needed because the PR adds a public security-sensitive compatibility mode and the remaining blockers include code defects, missing contributor proof, and maintainer contract decisions.

target.title: fix(write): add renameIdentity option for FUSE mounts with unstable inode numbers
target.body:
## Summary

This is a best-effort contribution based on my read of the codebase and the security model. Please feel free to redirect, adjust, or reject it entirely â€” I've tried to stay close to existing conventions but maintainers know this code better than I do.

Closes #32

---

### The problem

On rclone FUSE mounts, `rename(2)` invalidates the VFS cache entry for the destination path. Every subsequent path-based lookup then returns a freshly-minted inode number, making the post-rename `(dev, ino)` identity check in `runPinnedWriteFallback` always fail â€” even with a single process and zero concurrency. The check is correct by design on standard POSIX filesystems; it's the filesystem that doesn't honour the invariant the check relies on.

### Why the issue's suggested approach changed

The original report suggested a content-hash comparison as a direct replacement for the `(dev, ino)` check. During implementation, two problems came up:

**Content-only verification is weaker than it looks.** An adversary with the same UID and write access to the directory can race our rename with a file containing identical bytes, have that file accepted as ours, and then freely mutate it afterward â€” because we can't prove the resulting inode is the one we created, only that it currently holds the expected bytes. The attack requires predicting or copying our content, but the exact conditions that produce the bug (a slow, network-backed FUSE mount) also widen the race window, making the attack more feasible rather than less.

**A cooperative sidecar lock closes the realistic attack window.** `fs-safe` already has `withSidecarLock` / `withFileLock` as a documented cross-process coordination primitive. If the full write (not just the verification) runs under an exclusive sidecar lock, any other writer using the same lock protocol cannot interleave. Confirming content equality under those conditions is a meaningful guarantee: we know we are the only writer, and the bytes we see are the bytes we wrote. The residual risk â€” a same-UID process that deliberately ignores the lock â€” is explicitly outside fs-safe's documented per-call threat model and is the same residual risk that applies to any advisory-lock scheme.

### What this PR adds

**`renameIdentity?: RenameIdentityPolicy`** on `RootDefaults` and `RootWriteOptions`.

- `"strict"` (default, implicit): existing `(dev, ino)` check, behavior completely unchanged.
- `"verify-content-with-lock"`: acquires an exclusive sidecar lock at `{targetPath}.lock`, performs the full write under the lock, and verifies the result by SHA-256 comparing written bytes against the re-read target. The fd-based stat from the verification read is used as the returned identity so all subsequent checks (including `verifyAtomicWriteResult`) see a consistent value. Stale locks from crashed holders are detected via PID-liveness check and removed automatically rather than blocking indefinitely.

The lock acquisition happens **before** the write, not as a retry on failure. On a FUSE mount where the identity check always fails, a retry-on-failure design would silently do every write twice (a full temp-write + rename + failed check, then again under lock). Always locking when opted in costs one sidecar create/delete per write on normal filesystems, but avoids the wasted double-write on the affected mounts.

### Files changed

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `src/file-identity.ts` | `sha256Hex()` pure helper |
| `src/pinned-write.ts` | `onRenameIdentityMismatch` param; content-hash fallback branch inside `runPinnedWriteFallback` |
| `src/root-impl.ts` | `RenameIdentityPolicy` type; `renameIdentity` on `RootDefaults`/`RootWriteOptions`; `commitPinnedWriteInRoot` branching; `lockHolderIsAlive` for stale-lock detection |
| `src/root.ts`, `src/index.ts` | Re-export `RenameIdentityPolicy` |
| `docs/security-model.md` | New limitations table row |
| `docs/writing.md` | Updated `path-mismatch` entry; new FUSE section with example and security note |
| `test/rename-identity-policy.test.ts` | 7 new tests (see below) |

### Test plan

| Test | What it proves |
|---|---|
| A | Strict mode still throws `path-mismatch` on simulated inode churn â€” default behavior is unchanged |
| B | `verify-content-with-lock` succeeds when inode changes after rename but content matches |
| C | `verify-content-with-lock` still throws `path-mismatch` when content is replaced after rename â€” attacker content is not silently accepted |
| D | `renameIdentity` can be overridden per `write()` call independent of the root default |
| E | Works on a normal POSIX disk (lock acquired and released, write succeeds) |
| F | A stale lock left by a dead process (simulated via an impossible PID) is reclaimed automatically |
| G | A lock with no recognisable PID is not aggressively removed â€” the write fails rather than deleting an unverifiable lock |

The fix was verified end-to-end against the live rclone-mounted vault described in #32: strict mode continues to throw; `verify-content-with-lock` writes successfully, content matches, and the lock sidecar is released.
